fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a DOM assertion helper tool — basically a small utility that takes a JSON command over stdin and spits out a JSON result to stdout. The idea is that our QA folks are drowning in repetitive DOM inspection boilerplate and we want to give them a clean contract-based interface instead. Think of it like that adapter pattern we used on the login module last time — same separation idea, where the I/O layer just translates commands into calls to the real logic underneath.

The tool needs to handle things like: checking if elements exist (or don't), verifying text content (exact or fuzzy), inspecting attributes and their values, checking CSS classes, looking at form control values, and also disabled/focus states. Oh and there's some whitespace normalization thing we need — users keep complaining that copy-pasted text comparisons break because of invisible formatting differences.

For errors — if someone passes a weird target type that we don't understand, we need a normalized error response rather than a crash. The output format needs to be consistent and pretty-printed.

Also the whole thing should be structured properly, not just one giant file — our new engineer complained last time about god files. Reference the existing test contracts in the rcb_tests folder if you need to understand the expected shapes. Let me know if anything is unclear, I'm not super technical on the DOM side.

Quick follow-up after the team questions: for the whitespace piece, Tabs (\t), carriage returns (\r), newlines (\n), and repeated spaces must all be treated as ordinary whitespace. They should be collapsed into a single space, and the result must be trimmed at both ends. Non-whitespace characters must remain in their original order. See feature1_normalize_whitespace.json for the exact contract.

A little more color on how we describe matched nodes too, since that came up. When describing a collection (NodeList), join descriptions of up to the first five elements with ", ". If the collection is empty, output the string "empty NodeList". If there are more than five elements, append "... (+N more)" where N is the count of remaining elements beyond the first five. Each element in the list is described by its lowercase tag name, followed by #id if present, then .class names, then any non-id/non-class attributes. See feature2_node_description.json.

On presence checks, the default behavior is pretty simple: without a count argument, the presence assertion is satisfied when at least one element matches the selector. When arguments.count is provided, the assertion is satisfied only when the number of matching elements equals that exact count. The actual/expected/message fields must reflect the selector string as provided. See feature3_presence.json.

For focus state, the focused_state operation accepts a top-level 'focus' field in the JSON input, which is a CSS selector string identifying the currently focused element. The actual field in the assertion result should contain the described node (compact selector) of whatever element is currently focused. The expected field contains the target selector string. See feature13_focus_state.json.

Also confirming the bad target handling: when the target's 'kind' is not a recognized type (e.g., not 'selector' or 'all'), the output must be a JSON error object with three fields: error: "unexpected_target", target_type containing the kind string, and target_value containing the string representation of the value. No assertions array should be included. See feature14_unexpected_target_errors.json.

And one formatting thing we should be strict about because it affects every contract check: all JSON output must be pretty-printed with 2-space indentation and must end with a trailing newline character. This applies to all operations including errors. The exact format is JSON.stringify(obj, null, 2) + newline, or equivalent.

Last bit is structure. This should not turn into one giant file. The project must NOT be a single monolithic file. It requires a multi-file directory structure (e.g., src/, tests/) with clear separation: I/O adapter/routing layer, core domain logic, and output formatting must be in distinct modules. The core business logic must be fully decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing per the architecture constraints in start.md. Same idea as the Dependency Inversion Principle and Separation of Concerns described in start.md Architecture section: the execution adapter translates JSON stdin commands into idiomatic method calls to the core domain, which has no knowledge of I/O. If there’s any doubt on exact outputs, the JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ (feature1 through feature14) define the black-box stdin/stdout contract for every operation including exact field names, message phrasing, and error shapes.

trigger_keywords: absence with root scoping
how does root affect absence check
absence scoped to a container element
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The absence operation accepts an optional 'root' selector that scopes the querySelector to that subtree. When a root is provided, the query runs inside that root element rather than the whole document. When matching elements are found (i.e., assertion fails), the actual field should report how many exist using phrasing like "Element X exists once" or "Element X exists N times". See feature4_absence.json.

trigger_keywords: attribute matching with regex
how to use pattern for attribute value
regex expected value in attribute assertion
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The arguments.expected field for attribute_matches can be either a plain string for exact matching or an object with kind: "regex" and a pattern field for pattern matching. The attribute_present operation checks only that the attribute exists on the element, without any value comparison. See feature5_attribute_matching.json.

trigger_keywords: boolean attribute absence value
what value does a present boolean attribute show
attribute absent failure actual value for boolean attributes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the attribute_absent assertion fails because the attribute is present, the actual field must report the attribute's value. For boolean attributes (like 'required' or 'disabled') that are present without a value, the reported value must be the empty string "". See feature6_attribute_absence.json.

trigger_keywords: class assertion actual field format
what goes in actual for class_contains
class_absent actual value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For both class_contains and class_absent, the actual field must contain the full space-separated list of classes as they appear on the element (e.g., "secret-password-input foo"). The expected field contains just the single class name being checked. The message field uses the pattern "Element <selector> has CSS class \"<name>\"" for class_contains. See feature7_class_matching.json.

trigger_keywords: text_equals normalization
does text_equals normalize whitespace before comparing
text comparison whitespace handling
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes — for text_equals, text_contains, and text_matches, the element's text content (including all child text nodes) is extracted and then whitespace-normalized before comparison. The actual field in the assertion result contains the already-normalized text. See feature8_text_equality.json and the normalize_whitespace rules in feature1_normalize_whitespace.json.

trigger_keywords: regex text match expected field format
how is regex displayed in expected field
text_matches pattern output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the expected argument is a regex object (kind: "regex", pattern: "..."), the expected field in the assertion output must be formatted as "/pattern/" (i.e., wrapped in forward slashes). The message field must read "Element <selector> has text matching /pattern/". See feature9_text_pattern.json.

trigger_keywords: form value injection
how are input values set for testing
values field in operation payload
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The value_equals operation accepts a top-level 'values' array in the JSON input. Each entry has a 'selector' and a 'value'. The adapter must apply these values to the matching elements in the parsed document before running the assertion. This simulates user-entered form control values since static HTML doesn't carry them. See feature11_value_matching.json.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: normalized error response rather than a crash
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The specific error JSON shape defined in feature14_unexpected_target_errors.json: {"error": "unexpected_target", "target_type": "<kind>", "target_value": "<stringified value>"}
The top-level 'values' array in the value_equals operation payload (feature11_value_matching.json) that injects programmatic values into form elements before assertion, simulating user input

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but it's outside what I can clarify from the business side. Please check the test case files in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the precise expected input/output contract, and refer to the architecture section in start.md for structural requirements. If the test cases don't cover your specific scenario, use the closest analogous case as your guide.

feature12_disabled_state.json.description: Assert disabled state of selected controls

feature13_focus_state.json.description: Assert current focus and not-focus state

feature14_unexpected_target_errors.json.description: Normalize errors for unsupported assertion targets

feature2_node_description.json.description: Describe DOM targets as compact selectors

feature3_presence.json.description: Assert that selected elements exist with optional counts

feature4_absence.json.description: Assert that selected elements do not exist

feature7_class_matching.json.description: Assert CSS class membership and absence

feature8_text_equality.json.description: Assert complete text content after whitespace normalization
